mission.title: Install and Configure Openyurt on Kubernetes
mission.description: OpenYurt extends your native Kubernetes to edge environments, enabling seamless management of edge applications as if they were running in the cloud. Installing OpenYurt allows you to address challenges in cloud-edge orchestration, such as unreliable networking and edge device management.

steps.title: Add the OpenYurt Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the OpenYurt Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration to access the yurt-iot-dock chart.
```bash
helm repo add openyurt https://charts.openyurt.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the yurt-iot-dock component
steps.description:
Install the yurt-iot-dock component using Helm. This command will create a new namespace called 'openyurt' if it doesn't exist.
```bash
helm install yurt-iot-dock openyurt/yurt-iot-dock --namespace openyurt --create-namespace --version 1.6.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the yurt-iot-dock pods are running successfully in the 'openyurt' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n openyurt
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the deployment to set resource limits for the yurt-iot-dock component to ensure it runs efficiently.
```bash
kubectl edit deployment yurt-iot-dock -n openyurt
# Add the following lines under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
Forward a local port to the yurt-iot-dock service to access it from your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/yurt-iot-dock -n openyurt 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will show the yurt-iot-dock pods running in the 'openyurt' namespace, and you will be able to access the service via port 8080 on your local machine. Additionally, resource limits will be configured for optimal performance.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add openyurt https://charts.openyurt.io
helm repo update

kubectl edit deployment yurt-iot-dock -n openyurt
# Add the following lines under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the yurt-iot-dock release using Helm to remove the deployment from the cluster.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with OpenYurt.

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the 'openyurt' namespace and verify that all resources have been cleaned up.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Before upgrading, back up the current state of the yurt-iot-dock release.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to upgrade to the latest version.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check if the yurt-iot-dock pods are running the new version successfully.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue may occur if the application fails to start. Check the pod logs for errors using the following command: 
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n openyurt
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the service is not accessible, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. Verify the service status with: 
```bash
kubectl get svc -n openyurt
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are not starting due to resource constraints, check the resource requests and limits. Use: 
```bash
kubectl describe deployment yurt-iot-dock -n openyurt
```

troubleshooting.title: CRDs not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to CRDs, ensure that they are installed correctly. List CRDs with: 
```bash
kubectl get crds
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, and that Helm and kubectl are installed on your local machine.
